perturbation.original_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose Proprietary Information to a third party without the Disclosing Party's express written consent
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Receiving Party shall not disclose Proprietary Information to a third party without the Expressing Party's express written consent
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Disclosing Party" to "Expressing Party" in Section 2 while other sections still use "Disclosing Party" creates an ambiguity. It's unclear if 'Expressing Party' now has the same rights and obligations as the original 'Disclosing Party', particularly regarding consent for information disclosure. Other sections using "Disclosing Party" will be contradicted due to this change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. The Disclosing Party represents and warrants that it has the full right and authority to enter into this Agreement and to disclose the Proprietary Information to the Receiving Party pursuant to the terms hereof. The Disclosing Party makes no other representations or warranties concerning Proprietary Information, and hereby disclaims all other representations and warranties, whether express, implied, statutory or otherwise, including, without limitation, any warranties concerning the quality, accuracy or completeness of the Proprietary Information. In no event shall either Party be liable for indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive or STP NDA rev B
perturbation.justification: The change from "Disclosing Party" to "Expressing Party" in Section 2, while Section 10 still refers to "Disclosing Party", creates a contradiction as it introduces ambiguity regarding the rights and obligations related to proprietary information disclosure. Section 10 grants the "Disclosing Party" certain rights and warranties, and the change in terminology makes it unclear whether these rights now also apply to the "Expressing Party", thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Receiving Party shall safeguard Proprietary Information using at least the same degree of care the Receiving Party uses to protect its own proprietary information, but with no less than reasonable care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Receiving Party shall safeguard Proprietary Information using at least the same degree of care the Recipient uses to protect its own proprietary information, but with no less than reasonable care under the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Receiving Party" to "Recipient" only in this section introduces inconsistency. Throughout the NDA, 'Receiving Party' is used extensively. This singular change creates ambiguity about whether 'Recipient' is meant to be the same as, or different from, the 'Receiving Party' defined elsewhere, creating in text contradiction when reading the document in full.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees or contract labor personnel of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.justification: The change from "Receiving Party" to "Recipient" in the Changed Text introduces a contradiction with the Contradicted Text. The Contradicted Text repeatedly uses "Receiving Party" and outlines specific obligations and restrictions tied to that term. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that 'Receiving Party' is used consistently throughout the document. By introducing 'Recipient' in the Changed Text, it creates ambiguity whether 'Recipient' is the same as 'Receiving Party' (as defined elsewhere), or refers to a different entity, thus creating uncertainty about who is bound by the obligations outlined in the Contradicted Text. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition, as the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without properly defining them, leading to potentially conflicting interpretations of obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Receiving Party shall not use Proprietary Information except in furtherance of the Purpose for the mutual benefit of the Parties. The Receiving Party shall not use Proprietary Information for any other purpose , without the Disclosing Party's express prior written consent. The Receiving Party shall not make or permit to be made any copies of Proprietary Information except as required for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Receiving Entity shall not use Proprietary Information except in furtherance of the Purpose for the mutual benefit of the Parties. The Receiving Party shall not use Proprietary Information for any other purpose , without the Disclosing Party's express prior written consent. The Receiving Entity shall not make or permit to be made any copies of Proprietary Information except as required for the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Mixing 'Receiving Entity' and 'Receiving Party' within the same paragraph introduces an in-text contradiction. It becomes unclear whether the restrictions and permissions apply to both or if they are distinct entities, especially given that only 'Receiving Party' is formally defined in the WHEREAS clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. The Receiving Entity shall not use Proprietary Information except in furtherance of the Purpose for the mutual benefit of the Parties. The Receiving Party shall not use Proprietary Information for any other purpose , without the Disclosing Party's express prior written consent. The Receiving Entity shall not make or permit to be made any copies of Proprietary Information except as required for the Purpose.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'Receiving Entity' while retaining 'Receiving Party' within the same paragraph. The explanation states that this creates an in-text contradiction because only 'Receiving Party' is formally defined, making it unclear whether the restrictions apply to both. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction', creating a conflicting obligation within the document.

perturbation.original_text: 6. All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Storing Entity shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Receiving Party' with 'Storing Entity' creates ambiguity about who is responsible for returning or destroying proprietary information. Since 'Receiving Party' is defined and 'Storing Entity' is not, it's unclear if they refer to the same party. Since there is another "Receiving Party" mentioned on the section, that phrase now gets in text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Storing Entity shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Receiving Party' with 'Storing Entity' in the first sentence while 'Receiving Party' is still used in the rest of the paragraph. The explanation states this creates ambiguity because 'Storing Entity' is not defined, and the continued use of 'Receiving Party' causes in-text contradiction, as the passage becomes unclear about who is responsible for handling the Proprietary Information, directly impacting the enforceability of the obligation to return or destroy such information. This fits the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall expire five (5) year(s) after its effective date, provided, however, that either Party may terminate the Agreement on an earlier date, without cause, by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for the period of protection specified in paragraph 7 above.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall expire five (5) year(s) after its effective date, provided, however, that either Party may terminate the Agreement on an earlier date, without cause, by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. The Possessing Entity's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for the period of protection specified in paragraph 7 above.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Receiving Party' with 'Possessing Entity' in Section 13, concerning obligations after termination, creates a significant contradiction. It's unclear if the obligations now fall on a different entity than originally intended. This makes it uncertain who is legally bound to protect the information after the agreement ends, creating in text contradiction, since the term is not defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees or contract labor personnel of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Receiving Party' to 'Possessing Entity' introduces a contradiction with Section 5. Section 5 explicitly defines the responsibilities and limitations of the 'Receiving Party' regarding Proprietary Information access. By changing 'Receiving Party' to 'Possessing Entity' in Section 13, the contract creates uncertainty as to who is responsible for protecting the information after termination, directly conflicting with the defined responsibilities in Section 5. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition, as it uses a different term without defining it, leading to conflicting obligations.
